title: practical english usage - Part 335
content:
and not just 'said’  silently in the head.

Aloud is often used with the words read and think, to say that words are spoken, They were talking so loudly I couldn't hear myself think.

The  opposite is quietly.

Loudly is used (like loud) to talk about the strength of a noise.

520 loudly       and   aloud      _____ loosing weight. . .) not I must be losing weight - my clothes all feel loose. (     I must be Loose (pronounced /lu:s/) is an adjective (the opposite of tight).

Lose (pronounced /lu:z/) is an irregular verb (lose - lost - lost).

519  lose     and   loose ►    457 and look     forward,        hope,     expect          wait ►    575 and look     (a t), see       watch For other uses of look, see a good dictionary.

►     373.

For Look! used in persuading, ►    301 .2.       For the difference between look (at), watch and see, not (     . . .to look for Daniel.) I'm going to the station at three o'clock to fetch Daniel.

things are.

Fetch, not look for, is used in British English, if we  know where people or I spent ages looking for her before I found her.

Could you look after the kids while I go shopping?

These are not the same. Look after means 'take care of; look for means 'try to 4 look     after      and   look     for,    fetch I'm looking whether . . )' ‘ What are you doing? ‘ I'm looking to see whether these batteries are OK.

Could you see if Anna's in the kitchen? (     Could you look if Anna's Before if or whether, we  use see or look to see, not look.

3  not   followed       by   if not Look! It's changing colour. (     Look at!...) A  preposition is not used when there is no object.

The boss looked at me angrily. (     The boss looked at me angry.) Before an object, a preposition is necessary (usually at).

When look means 'direct one’ s eyes’ , it is used with adverbs, not adjectives.

2 ordinary       verb    (= 'direct    one's    eyes') Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  521  magic and magical Andrew's going to divorce Carola.

She married a builder, (     She married with a builder.) Before a direct object, marry and divorce are used without prepositions.

2 no   preposition        before     object After three very unhappy years they divorced.

Although she had many lovers, she never married.

In a more formal style, marry and divorce are preferred.

The Robinsons are getting divorced.

Lulu and Joe got married last week. (Lulu and Joe married ...

and divorce when there is no object.

In an informal style, get married and get divorced are more common than marry 1  get    married/divorced 523 marry       and   divorce ►     464 and male         masculine ►     435 and make         do For causative structures with make (e.g. He. made, us laugh), ►     107.

’ The soup's good.' ’ Yes, I make it with lots of garlic.' To  mention one of several materials (e.g. in cooking), we  can use make with.

He made all the window-frames out of oak; it took a long time.

When we  talk about the process of manufacture, we  can also use out of not Paper is made from wood, (     Paper is made of wood-.) we  often use make from.

When  a material is changed into a completely different form to make something, All our furniture is made of wood.

Most things seem to be made of plastic these days.

We   usually say that something is made of a particular material.

522 make:     prepositions It was a magical experience.

like 'mysterious', 'wonderful' or 'exciting'.

Magical is sometimes used instead of magic, especially in metaphorical senses the magic word a magic carpet a magic wand (= a magician's stick) Magic is the more common word, and is used in a number of fixed expressions.

521  magjc       and   magical         ________________ 526 vocabulary •         mean Did you mean Jack to pay for everybody?

Sorry -1 didn't mean to interrupt you.

(object)          + infinitive.

Mean (= intend, plan) can be followed by 3 structures not What's your opinion? (     What's your meaning?) tViil      U/Crl  •  • fill     VI       •|  J 9 fill not I think that Labour will win the next election, (     Fm ean that Labour They  are not usually used for 'think' or 'opinion'.
